title: On the Completions of General Period Mappings
speaker: Haohua Deng
abstract: Constructing completions of period mappings with significant geometric and Hodge-theoretical meaning is an important topic in Hodge theory and its applications. There are rich theories for the classical case in which the period domain is Hermitian symmetric. In this talk I will talk about recent breakthroughs in the non-classical settings, particularly the generalized toroidal completion in the sense of Ash--Mumford--Raport--Tai and Kato--Nakayama--Usui. Joint works with Colleen Robles and Jacob Tsimerman.
tags: Workshop on Recent Developments in Hodge Theory and O-minimality

proof_stages.label: Griffiths extension problem for period maps
proof_stages.summary: A smooth projective family over a quasiprojective base gives an integral polarized variation of Hodge structure and a period map, and compactifying the base raises Griffiths' problem of extending this map so that boundary points still encode limiting Hodge data in a geometrically meaningful category.

proof_stages.label: Classical symmetric-domain benchmark
proof_stages.summary: In classical cases where the period domain is Hermitian symmetric, Satake-Baily-Borel and toroidal compactifications provide the expected extensions and clarify what boundary information is retained or lost.

proof_stages.label: Nonclassical obstruction and need for a new category
proof_stages.summary: For nonsymmetric period domains there are no suitable automorphic functions and arithmetic quotients need not carry compatible algebraic or analytic structures, so the classical compactification problem must be reformulated in a broader setting.

proof_stages.label: Nilpotent orbits and Kato-Usui logarithmic spaces
proof_stages.summary: Schmid's nilpotent orbit theorem identifies the limiting Hodge-theoretic boundary data, and Kato-Nakayama-Usui use collections of nilpotent cones and Gamma-compatible weak fans to build logarithmic spaces into which period maps extend.

proof_stages.label: Fan-construction bottleneck for full period maps
proof_stages.summary: Applying the Kato-Usui theorem globally requires constructing compatible fans, which is automatic for curve bases and classical symmetric cases and recently manageable for surface bases, but generally fails in dimension at least three because higher-dimensional monodromy cones have uncontrolled intersections.

proof_stages.label: Generalized toroidal completion of the period image
proof_stages.summary: The new approach replaces completion of the full quotient period domain by completion of the Stein-factorized period image, using a finite local cover with local Kato-Usui extensions whose Stein factorizations patch to an algebraic-space generalized toroidal compactification.

proof_stages.label: Applications and comparison questions
proof_stages.summary: The construction yields a new proof and sharper boundary description of algebraicity of Hodge loci and suggests future comparison with generalized Baily-Borel compactifications.

examples.name: Torelli/Jacobian map and its compactifications
examples.note: The map from the moduli space of smooth genus-g curves to principally polarized abelian varieties, together with Satake-Baily-Borel and toroidal compactifications, illustrates the classical form of an extended period map.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the Torelli/Jacobian period map through Satake–Baily–Borel and toroidal compactifications and traces how stable curves are sent under the extensions.

passes.reason: The Jacobian/Torelli period map is a classical positive model landing in a Siegel locally symmetric quotient with standard compactifications.

examples.name: One-nodal genus-two curve
examples.note: A stable genus-two curve with elliptic normalization and two preimages of the node shows concretely that the Satake-Baily-Borel extension remembers only the Jacobian of the normalization while the toroidal boundary also records the Abel-Jacobi class of the difference of the two points.
examples.engagement_reason: The one-nodal genus-two curve is worked through explicitly: its normalization is identified as an elliptic curve, its image in the Baily–Borel boundary is described, and the toroidal compactification is said to recover the points via p−q in Pic^0.

passes.reason: The one-nodal genus-two curve is a singular boundary degeneration used to show the Baily-Borel compactification forgets the node-preimage data.
a one-nodal genus-two stable curve is a singular boundary degeneration of a smooth curve, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
A one-nodal genus-two stable curve is a singular boundary degeneration, used illustratively to compare Satake and toroidal boundary data.
A one-nodal genus-two stable curve is a singular boundary degeneration of a smooth genus-two curve, used to illustrate what compactifications remember.
A one-nodal genus-two curve is a singular boundary degeneration obtained by identifying two points on its elliptic normalization.
A one-nodal stable genus-two curve is a singular boundary degeneration with lower-genus normalization.
A one-nodal stable genus-two curve is a boundary degeneration of smooth curves, used to illustrate information lost by SBB and recovered toroidally.
A singular boundary degeneration of a genus-two curve used to show SBB remembers only the normalization/Jacobian and loses the node-gluing data.
The one-nodal genus-two curve is a singular boundary degeneration used to show that the SBB compactification forgets the gluing-point data.

examples.name: Calabi-Yau threefolds and surfaces of general type
examples.note: These moduli problems illustrate the nonclassical situation where the relevant period domain is generally not a Hermitian symmetric domain, especially for surfaces with h^{2,0} greater than one.
examples.engagement_reason: Calabi–Yau threefolds and surfaces of general type are cited as nonclassical examples, with only general facts about their period domains stated.

passes.reason: Calabi-Yau threefolds and general-type surfaces are ordinary nonclassical cases used to show locally symmetric compactification theory does not cover general period maps.

examples.name: Base curve monodromy rays
examples.note: For a one-dimensional base, the logarithms of local monodromies give rays whose translates either coincide or meet only at the origin, making the fan condition transparent and contrasting with the higher-dimensional obstruction.
examples.engagement_reason: For a base curve the speaker concretely constructs the relevant fan from one-dimensional local monodromy rays and checks why their gamma-conjugates satisfy the fan property.

passes.reason: For a curve base the monodromy cones are one-dimensional rays, minimal/extreme cones whose translates still form a fan in the positive case.
for a curve base the local monodromy cones are one-dimensional minimal/extreme rays, serving as the easy positive fan case.
For a base curve the monodromy cones are one-dimensional rays, the minimal nonzero/extreme cone case that automatically forms a fan.
For a one-dimensional base the monodromy cones are minimal nonzero rays, making the fan condition an easy base case rather than an obstruction.
For a curve base the nilpotent monodromy cones are one-dimensional rays, the minimal extreme cone case that makes the fan condition automatic.
For a curve base the monodromy cones are one-dimensional rays, the minimal easy fan case.
For a curve base the monodromy cones reduce to minimal one-dimensional rays, giving the easy fan case rather than an obstruction.
The curve-base case gives one-dimensional monodromy cones, i.e. extremal rays, making the fan condition automatic.
For a base curve the monodromy cones are one-dimensional rays, the minimal and extremal cone case where the fan condition is automatic.
For a base curve the local monodromy cones are one-dimensional rays, the minimal nontrivial cone case making the fan condition automatic.
For a curve base the monodromy cones are minimal one-dimensional extreme rays giving a positive easy fan case.
For a base curve the monodromy cones are one-dimensional rays, the minimal case in which the fan condition is automatic.

examples.name: Loops of boundary strata and global monodromy
examples.note: The audience-raised scenario of boundary components looping and producing extra monodromy explains why local extensions are insufficient and forces the global compatibility and patching data used later.
examples.engagement_reason: Loops of boundary strata/global monodromy are invoked to motivate why local extensions must be patched using global monodromy, but no specific loop or monodromy calculation is carried out.

passes.reason: Loops of boundary strata create global monodromy effects, showing purely local monodromy data is insufficient for global patching.
